item.title: Green Door, Santa Maria della Salute
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: Sargent knew Venice the way none of the visitors before him did — he had family roots there, spoke the dialect, and returned almost yearly. So when he painted the Salute, the church we have now seen from Guardi's middle distance and Turner's porch, he did not paint the view at all. He pulled his gondola up to the church's flank and painted its steps, its shadowed marble, and a workaday green door — the Venice of side entrances, known only to people who no longer need the front one. Watercolor was his off-duty medium, kept for himself while oil portraits paid the bills, and the freedom shows: washes dragged wet into wet, the paper's white left bare to stand for sun-struck stone, a whole architecture of shadow mixed from blue and amber in a single pass. This is the intimate answer to our question about light on water: get close enough, and you stop painting the city's face. You start painting its skin.
item.lookFor: Find the places where Sargent painted nothing at all — bare paper doing the work of brilliant marble. The strongest light in the picture is the absence of paint.
